design: PARAMETERIZED FORM SCHEMAS: ~11 compiler-known form generators (seq/ring/table/btree/pool/region/bytescan/view/cursor/conc_queue/shard) with once-verified lowering templates, closed policy enums, pure plug-points, an optimizer fact sheet per schema; plus a checked-library alg kit, five builtin-TCB facades (alloc/sync/threads/io/rcu_table), and two language-primitive bundles (machine core, margin_slice/wbuf). Claims PAR or WIN on 48 of 50 demand-map scenarios.
lens: PERFORMANCE HOLES: common workloads where no blessed spelling plausibly reaches the named SOTA, plus performance predictability and the real power of the optimizer-facts channel.

findings.title: Multi-node link surgery is untypeable at the claimed cost: intrusive lists, LRU promote, timer-wheel cancel, and tree detach all rest on holding two mutable spots in one pool, which the affine rules forbid
findings.detail: The coverage rows for intrusive links, LRU, timer wheel, and navigable trees claim '2-4 stores on already-fetched lines' (Linux list_del parity, lru-crate 20-30 ns get-with-promote, tokio-wheel 10-20 ns cancel). But link editing is USER SOURCE over pool fields, and per OWN-12 two &uniq arguments whose resolved places overlap are an error — pool.get_uniq(&uniq p, h) borrows the WHOLE pool, so &uniq to prev and next can never coexist; the design's own 'disjoint uniq split' delta only covers constant-index slice chunks, not runtime handles h1≠h2. The only spelling is sequential: get(node) to copy its 8B links, then get_uniq(prev), get_uniq(next), get_uniq(node) one at a time — 3-4 separate pool ops, each paying bounds check + generation compare + an Option/stale match arm (~4-6 instructions and 1-2 branches each) versus C's 2 loads + 2 stores (~6 instructions total). No fact can elide the repeated validation because each &uniq op conservatively invalidates liveness facts about the pool. Expected: unlink at 3-5x the C instruction count, pushing LRU get-with-promote to ~1.3-1.5x the lru-crate target and wheel cancel toward the top of or past its 10-20 ns band; the doubly-linked discipline is also exactly where a modest writer will drift into remove+reinsert (the map's named 10-30x fail shape). Evidence to settle: (1) attempt to type the doubly-linked unlink in the checker as specified — predict it requires 4 sequential whole-pool borrows; (2) build the composed LRU and timer wheel and bench against lru 0.12 and tokio's wheel. Fix direction: unlink/splice/link-field ops must live INSIDE the pool's verified template (schema ops with a two-handle disjointness check discharged by the template's own invariant), not in user source.

findings.title: Arena cross-references do not exist in the language: the region schema's bumpalo/rustc-arena PAR claim assumes storable same-region references that neither the spec nor the design's language deltas provide
findings.detail: Σ6 claims 'same-arena cross-references are ordinary whitefoot region facts — the hard checker part already exists.' The kernel spec says otherwise: arena<'r,T> is a single-value region-bounded owned pointer (STOR-1/STOR-2/STOR-4, created by arena_new(v), accessed via deref), i.e. a region-tied box — an ownership TREE primitive. Borrows are binding/parameter MODES, not field types; struct fields take `type` only, and the settled rule is that borrows cannot be stored in data. So a node graph with parent pointers or shared subterms — the entire reason rustc/bumpalo arenas exist — has no spelling: you get either a strict ownership tree (no parent links, no sharing) or pool handles. With handles, allocation parity survives (~2-5 ns insert) but traversal parity dies: every hop pays bounds + generation validation (~4-6 extra instructions) on L1-resident walks where the load itself is ~4 cycles, i.e. ~1.5-2x on AST/DOM traversal versus the C/bumpalo pointer-deref baseline the map requires (within 10-20%). The 'region reincarnation' delta addresses reset, not reference storage; no delta adds a storable &'r-into-region reference type, and adding one (interior references in data, with mutation aliasing) is precisely the Rust-scale inference the frontend-simple ruling forbids. Evidence: grep of kernel-spec-v0.6 sections 5/STOR (done — confirms only ownership-tree arena pointers); then attempt the 1M-node AST-with-parent-pointers exemplar and measure traversal vs a C arena. Either the design adds a real (and hard) stored-region-reference delta, or the region row's 'build-then-drop object GRAPH' claim must be demoted to trees-plus-handles with the traversal tax stated.

findings.title: The alg kit's WIN/PAR claims require inductive, loop-carried fact discharge that the shipped requires-prologue engine cannot do — checked-source sort/heap/partition will keep their bounds checks
findings.detail: The alg kit is 'ordinary checked whitefoot source' with 'bounds inside all loops discharged by seq/slice facts.' But the measured mechanism (27/27 on base64, 1.71x) is a boundary-entry prologue eliding checks DOMINATED by facts verified once at entry. The kit's hot loops need more: ipnsort's branchless partition stores at data-dependent indices (dst + (x<pivot) offsets) whose in-boundedness is an inductive invariant over two moving cursors; 4-ary heap sift needs 4i+4 < len under a loop-varying i; binary search needs lo+((hi-lo)>>1) < len maintained across iterations. These are loop-invariant/inductive obligations, not entry-dominated ones, and the checker is ruled frontend-simple with no fixpoints or inference (checker_story says so explicitly). Rust's std sort is unsafe internally for exactly this reason; published safe-only (bounds-checked) sort ports run ~1.2-2x slower than ipnsort on random u64. So the sort row's WIN (and the radix path, whose counting-scatter writes are also data-dependent) degrades to 1.2-1.6x BEHIND the preregistered bar, and heap/search PAR claims inherit the same gap. E2's pass band only tests the easy case (post-reserve counted push loop — a genuinely entry-dominated fact). Evidence: write the branchless partition in whitefoot, inspect asm for residual per-store bounds branches; port ipnsort to fully-safe Rust as the cost model. Fix direction: either an explicit inductive-fact judgment (spec growth, feasibility risk) or move the kit's inner loops into verified templates like the schemas — which the design should say now, since it changes the TCB/proof workload.

findings.title: Plug-point functions cannot capture context, so keyed-sort-by-external-column (argsort) and the interner's view_eq are inexpressible as designed
findings.detail: Plugs are 'pure whitefoot functions' with empty write/alloc rows; there are no closures, and borrows cannot be stored in env structs. Two demand-map-central uses need a plug that reads external state: (1) argsort — sort a permutation of u32 indices by keys in ANOTHER array (the backbone of columnar engines and this project's own P2 SoA tapes): the extractor must read the column, but a pure fn of the index alone cannot reach it; (2) Σ3's probe_view(key_view, view_eq) for interning: entries store spans into a region byte store, so view_eq must resolve span→bytes, which requires the arena — not present in the stated plug signature. Workarounds: materialize (key, idx) pairs into a seq (16B/elem: 16MB extra traffic + an extra pass at 1M rows, then a second pass to extract — ~1.3-2x plus allocation vs sort_unstable_by over indices), or redesign the op signatures to thread an explicit context argument through every plug call (a real design change with its own aliasing question: the context borrow must coexist with the table's own &uniq during insert-triggered probes). Evidence: try to write the rustc-interner exemplar's view_eq and the columnar argsort with the signatures as published — predict both fail to type; then measure the materialize workaround vs Rust sort_by_key over indices. The interning PAR row and any columnar-sort claim are unproven until the context-passing story is written.

findings.title: Fixed op tables turn every missing operation into a multi-pass slow path with a quarters-long fix latency: retain/drain, get_many_mut, bulk load, remove-during-iterate are absent and their workarounds cost 2-3x
findings.detail: R3 one-spelling + sealed invariants means users cannot add ops against a schema's internals, and the catalog-finding process is the only remedy. Concrete common gaps in the published op tables: table has no retain/drain (the session-expiry sweep every server runs: the workaround — iterate, collect expired keys into a seq, then remove one-by-one — re-hashes every expired key and allocates, ~2-3x hashbrown's retain which walks ctrl bytes once); no get_many_mut (moving a value between two keys); btree has no bulk-load-from-sorted (building an index from sorted input is O(n) append-leaves in SOTA vs n·log n inserts here, ~3-5x on load phases); ring has no rotate/make_contiguous. Each gap is individually small; structurally they compound: hashbrown/BTreeMap accreted these ops over years of demand, and in Rust a blocked user drops to unsafe or a fork the same week, while here the program ships below par until a catalog release. Evidence: diff hashbrown 0.15 + std BTreeMap public API against Σ3/Σ4 op tables, count demand-map-implied ops missing; bench the retain workaround at 100K sessions/10% expiry. Fix: audit each schema's op table against the reference library's full API before freezing, and define an expedited op-addition lane (ops are inside the verified template, so each addition re-runs the template proof — budget that).

findings.title: bytescan's closed vocabulary covers exactly the shipped instances; the first unshipped format — including memmem, which the demand map names — drops the writer to raw SIMD where W1-grade code lands well under par
findings.detail: Σ7's pred enum (eq1-3, range_set, nibble_table) and carry enum (none, clmul_quote_mask, utf8_range_state) parameterize the named instances (memchr-class, utf8, json stage-1, base64). Missing from both the schema and the alg kit: substring search (memmem) — which the demand map's F5-1 verdict explicitly requires as the second catalog entry ('rare-byte heuristic + Two-Way fallback layered on the same scan primitive') and which ripgrep-class tools live on; also CSV quote-state carries, HTML tokenization, and any custom framing whose carry isn't one of the two shipped states. The fallback is the machine-core vector primitives — checked source, but expert SIMD authorship: a modest writer's memmem (naive memchr-then-memcmp without rare-byte selection or Two-Way) is O(n·m)-degenerate on periodic inputs and plausibly ≤0.5x memchr::memmem GB/s even on benign ones. Compounding: the design itself flags the NEON movemask bridge (~0.7x risk on ARM) for every Σ7 instance. Evidence: (a) add-or-admit memmem — check whether rare-byte+Two-Way decomposes into the closed vocabulary (predict: the positional shift logic does not); (b) have a mid-tier model write memmem in the vector core and bench vs memchr 2.7 on the sliceslice corpus. Cheap fix: ship memmem as a named alg-kit entry now; state the fallback tier honestly as expert-only.

findings.title: Silent par decay is a designed-in failure mode: the legal-but-wrong spellings compile clean and lose 2-10x, and the design's only mitigations (decision table, aspirational lint) are untested against the writers it targets
findings.detail: The design admits the headline case ('par decays without any error appearing' when writers fall off affine-token idioms into copy-out/clone shapes). The cliff inventory is wider and each entry is unguarded: (1) copy-out-then-insert instead of entry(): 2x hash+probe on every counter/cache RMW; (2) seq inline=N owner moves memcpy the full inline footprint even when spilled — inline=16 of a 64B T is 1KB per move, and affine style (move consumes whole bindings, no reborrow) moves owners far more often than C++ passes SmallVector by reference, so the SmallVector-class 'win' knob can invert into a per-call 1KB tax with zero diagnostics; (3) hasher=foldhash on sequential u64 keys where int_identity is right, or vice versa on adversarial keys: 2-3x either direction; (4) conc_queue<mpmc> where endpoints are actually SPSC: 3-5x per the map, and nothing nudges the writer toward the affine spsc tokens. In C++/Rust these mistakes are also possible but reviewable against open internals; here the catalog IS the performance story, so mischoice is the dominant residual risk and the promised lint doesn't exist yet. Evidence: run the W1 experiment the design skips — have mid-tier models write the LRU, interner, and log-pipeline exemplars from the taught decision table only, and measure the achieved-vs-par distribution; preregister a threshold (e.g. ≥80% of attempts within 1.3x) as the writability gate alongside E1-E3.

overall: The stance is rescuable — the schema/template architecture is the right answer for the self-contained forms (seq, ring, table, btree, view, conc_queue are genuinely SOTA-shaped and the verification bet is at least honestly priced) — but the coverage table systematically overclaims wherever par depends on USER-SOURCE COMPOSITION across forms rather than on a template: link surgery (LRU/timers/trees/intrusive lists), arena object graphs, alg-kit inner loops, and context-consuming plugs all hit the same wall, namely that the affine no-reborrow checker plus a boundary-only fact engine cannot reproduce what SwissTable/bumpalo/ipnsort do with internal unsafe, and roughly a quarter of the PAR verdicts quietly assume it can. The single change that matters most: relocate the composite hot paths INSIDE the verified templates — ship pool link-edit/splice ops, table retain/drain and context-threaded probe_view, btree bulk-load, alg-kit loops as proved templates rather than checked source, and either a real stored-region-reference delta or an honest handle-tax demotion of the region row — accepting the larger proof workload up front instead of discovering, one exemplar at a time, that the blessed user-level spelling exists but runs 1.3-3x off par with no compile-time signal.

design: design:schema — PARAMETERIZED FORM SCHEMAS (11 schemas + alg kit + 5 builtin-TCB facades + 2 primitive bundles, verified lowering templates, closed policy enums, fact-sheet export)
lens: SAFETY & TCB — authority forgery at API boundaries, invalidation across mutation, token/guard typeability under no-reborrow/no-borrows-in-data, trap and teardown paths, reclamation contracts, honesty of the TCB inventory, decidability of new checker judgments

findings.title: Resumable-access tokens (entry token, btree cursor, shard guard) are untypeable under the stated checker rules, and every workaround reopens invalidation UAF
findings.detail: The design's own source shapes require values that resume access to a container position later: table.entry -> vacant(tok) then fill(tok, id); `let c = ByDeadline.range(&t, lo, hi)` then btree.next(&uniq c); shard.enter -> guard exposing &uniq inner; mutex.lock -> guard exposing &uniq T. The digest's settled rules say borrows CANNOT be stored in structs/data, a unique borrow is consumed by a call, and there is no reborrowing. So each token either (a) stores borrow-authority in a value — forbidden by the kernel — or (b) stores only a raw slot index/node position while the container binding stays reachable, in which case the classic invalidation attack is well-typed: between entry(k) and fill(tok,v), call insert(&uniq t, k2, v2) and trigger growth/rehash; fill then writes key/value through a stale index into the old (freed) allocation or stamps a ctrl byte for the wrong slot — UAF/OOB through the safe facade, violating I_table with zero trusted records in the blame path. Identically: a btree range cursor across a rebalancing insert; a pool handle-based navigation is fine (handles are checked), but the cursor forms are not. The checker_story claims 'existing affine machinery handles entry tokens, guards, endpoints, and cursors without extension' — this is true only for owning endpoint tokens (conc_queue), false for every access token. A new kernel judgment is required ('while tok/guard/cursor is live, the container binding is frozen — no op may take &uniq or & to it'), it appears nowhere in the 18 language_deltas, it costs rules against the 48-rule budget, and it must also cover condvar: wait must consume the guard and reissue it, else the guard's exposed &uniq T survives across the unlocked window while another thread mutates T. Evidence to settle: write the typing derivations for the design's own four source_shape lines under kernel v0.6 plus the listed deltas; they either fail to typecheck (design incomplete as specified) or admit an interleaved mutation between token issue and token use (unsound).

findings.title: margin_slice introduced from seq spare capacity exposes uninitialized reads
findings.detail: buffer core says margin_slice is 'produced only by allocation-side forms (seq.reserve_margin, buffer_with_margin)' and the source shape shows `seq.view_margin<8>(&buf)`. A seq's bytes between len and cap are uninitialized by definition (growth allocates raw capacity; only buffer<T> guarantees full init at creation). A margin_slice guarantees W READABLE slop elements past logical end, and cursor/bytescan perform wide loads into that slop (the whole point: varint 8B loads, unmasked vector tails). Reading seq spare capacity is therefore an uninitialized read — exactly the T1 class declared unrepresentable — and since these loads feed the optimizer-visible fact machinery, LLVM-level undef/poison can propagate into miscompiles, not just garbage values. The fix is cheap but must be in the introduction rule: reserve_margin/view_margin must zero (or otherwise provably initialize) the W slop elements at introduction — which then has to be reconciled with the adjacent wbuf claim of 'zero memset in the path by construction' when margin outputs and wbuf compose in the decode path (lz_copy wild-stores into slop, then the committed prefix advances over partially wild-written bytes). Evidence: state the precise introduction rule for each margin_slice producer and exhibit, for each, why every one of the W slop elements is initialized before any margin-consuming load is typeable; asm-inspect where the memset landed and whether the wbuf zero-store claim still holds.

findings.title: rcu_table and publish/snapshot views have no typeable lifetime anchor — the pin is not reified at the API
findings.detail: rcu_table.get's source shape is `match Routes.get(&r, k) { some(v) => ... }` with v described as 'a pinned-scope view' — but no pin object exists in the source. If the epoch pin is taken and released inside get, v dangles the instant get returns and the deferred-reclamation kernel may free the entry while v is live: use-after-free delivered by the sealed builtin, precisely the forgery-at-the-boundary this lens hunts. The language has no shared-ownership primitive (box is unique, no Arc, no 'static), so the only sound shape is an explicit guard-scoped pin region: `let g = Routes.pin(&r); Routes.get(&g, k)` with v's region tied to g — which is a new region-introduction rule (uncounted in the deltas) and collides with finding 1's token problem. publish/snapshot has the identical hole: snapshot returns a view of the currently-published frozen value; the writer then publishes a replacement; the old value needs refcount- or epoch-deferred reclamation and the snapshot view needs the same anchor region — 'arc-swap-class ~2 ns read' is quoted without the arc. Additionally the TCB inventory omits the reclamation executor entirely: which thread runs deferred frees and the compiler-derived drops of evicted V values, under what ordering guarantees, and with what proof — that context is trusted code and is not on the enumerated TCB list. Evidence: write the return-region typing of rcu_table.get and publish.load; produce the TCB line item for the deferred-drop executor or show reclamation never runs user-type drops.

findings.title: io.ring teardown with in-flight kernel DMA is unspecified — the loan token protects source access, not the allocation
findings.detail: The linear wbuf-loan token makes the buffer unreachable from source until completion — correct as far as it goes. But nothing stated constrains the RING's own lifetime: the ring is an ordinary owned value with a compiler-derived drop. Sequence: submit_read(&uniq ring, fd, wbuf_loan); ring goes out of scope (or is moved and dropped, or its enclosing region ends) before await1; drop frees the ring and — because the loan token is dropped with it — the wbuf backing store; the kernel then completes the read into recycled memory. That is memory corruption invisible to the type system, the exact io_uring cancellation-soundness problem that plagues Rust async (where Vec-backed buffers + future cancellation forced the registered-buffer/owned-buffer redesigns in tokio-uring/glommio). The TCB must own a teardown protocol — blocking drop that drains/cancels synchronously (io_uring cancel + wait for CQEs), or registered buffers whose ownership is transferred to the ring — and none is stated; nor is the interaction with scoped-spawn/trap exits while loans are outstanding (if a trap on one thread unwinds a scope containing the ring, the same free-under-DMA fires unless trap teardown also drains). Evidence: specify io.ring's drop semantics as a TCB contract; adversarial test: submit a large read, immediately drop the ring, immediately reallocate, and watch for the kernel's write landing in the new allocation.

findings.title: Trap semantics are incompatible with the concurrency kernels as described — 'join propagates failure trap' implies non-fatal traps, but no abandoned-state story exists
findings.detail: threads&par says 'join returns value or propagates failure trap', which means a trapping thread does NOT abort the process (otherwise there is nothing to propagate). Then every kernel needs defined semantics for a thread that traps mid-protocol, and none is given: (a) trap while holding a mutex guard — the lock word is left held and the protected T may be mid-mutation (the guard exposed &uniq T); the next locker either deadlocks forever or observes a T violating its invariants — poisoning semantics are required and absent; (b) trap between a Vyukov slot claim (tail fetch_add done) and the slot's sequence publication — the queue wedges permanently for all threads and the slot holds a half-moved T whose drop obligation is now owned by no one (double-drop or leak depending on teardown); (c) trap between Chase-Lev bottom decrement and the last-element CAS — a task value is in limbo. Either traps are process-fatal (consistent with 'traps-to-the-boundary', but then the join contract and 'propagates failure' wording must be fixed and scoped-spawn failure handling redesigned), or every trusted kernel and every &uniq-exposing guard needs an abandoned-state proof — a substantial unlisted TCB obligation. Evidence: one normative paragraph deciding trap scope (thread vs process); if thread-scoped, loom/GenMC runs with trap injection between claim and publish in each of the five kernels, plus a specified mutex-poisoning rule.

findings.title: Fact sheets are optimizer-trusted but only human-reviewed — which silently reclassifies all eleven 'non-TCB' templates as TCB
findings.detail: The template proof discharges T1/T2 for the template's OWN loads/stores. But exported facts (cap_slack(n), growth_slack(n), validated(L), sorted(view)) elide checks in USER code via the requires engine: if table under some exotic (K,V,hasher,N) tuple exports cap_slack(n) when only n-1 slots exist, a checked-source counted push loop writes one past capacity — an OOB write in fully checked code with no trusted record anywhere in the blame path. The design routes fact sheets through 'hostile adversarial review' (human), not through the machine-checked artifact. A human-reviewed channel whose failure mode is silent check-elision IS trust — so the headline 'eleven sequential templates are NOT in the TCB' is only true if each fact clause is machine-proved as a lemma of I_Σ, quantified over the full parameter space, inside the same shipped proof artifact. Separately, the artifact checker itself is undersold: re-verifying parametric separation-logic-grade heap proofs for SwissTable/B-tree templates ('in < 60 s') is not 'a small checker' — it is a proof checker for a frame-reasoning logic, it sits squarely in the TCB, and its own size/verification story is absent from the inventory. Evidence: amend E1's pass criteria to require machine-checked fact-soundness lemmas (I_Σ ⇒ every exported fact, ∀ params); measure the artifact checker's trusted line count against the 'small' claim.

findings.title: alg-kit 'ordinary checked source' likely does not typecheck: in-place sort/heap over Movable T needs swap/hole manipulation the kernel forbids
findings.detail: sort(uniq slice) and the 4-ary heap sift are claimed as checked whitefoot source, 'no TCB'. In-place partition and sift require swapping slice elements: for non-Copy Movable T that means moving a value out of a slot, leaving a hole, and refilling it — but the digest rules out partial-move recovery and reinitialization of moved-out places, and no slice-swap or replace primitive appears in the language deltas. So either (a) alg kit is restricted to Copy elements (defensible — sort (key, handle) pairs — but then the coverage claims for 'sorting owned runs' of arbitrary T are overstated and should say so), or (b) a swap/replace-on-uniq-slice rule must be added (spec growth, uncounted), or (c) sort gets reclassified as a Σ template (fine for safety, but it falsifies the checked-vs-template accounting the stance advertises). This fails closed — the code simply won't compile — so it is a classification/coverage defect, not a soundness hole. Evidence: attempt the typing derivation of the branchless-partition inner loop (conditional swap of s[i], s[j]) under kernel v0.6 + listed deltas for a Movable non-Copy T.

findings.title: Two new judgments flirt with proof search: disjoint uniq-slice split over runtime values, and value-dependent validated(L) facts
findings.detail: (a) The delta says the split judgment works 'by index arithmetic on constants/lengths', but par.for_chunks splits by a runtime length and runtime-ish chunk size into k chunks where k = ceil(len/chunk): proving pairwise disjointness of [i*c,(i+1)*c) for symbolic i≠j is arithmetic entailment, not constant comparison. Decidable frontend-simply ONLY if the rule is a single blessed combinator (the checker trusts for_chunks's fixed chunking scheme wholesale, proved once like a template) rather than a general split judgment — the text is ambiguous between the two, and the general reading smuggles in arithmetic reasoning the settled frontend-simple ruling forbids. (b) view's layout sublanguage includes 'length-prefixed sections': the validated(L,bytes) fact then depends on runtime values read from the buffer (section lengths), and every later residual-check-free access at a data-dependent offset must be connected to the validation without dataflow analysis — sound only if the fact is threaded as an affine witness token per validated view (which reconnects to finding 1's token problem), not looked up by the optimizer. (c) select.strict's 'verified never-branch' check runs post-optimization; it must be a hard build-failure gate with a stability story across backend versions, else it degrades into the same LLVM cmov wobble it claims to fix. Evidence: write each judgment as syntax-directed inference rules; any premise requiring arithmetic entailment beyond constant folding or any fact consumed at a distance from its introduction without a threaded witness is a decidability breach.

overall: The stance is rescuable and the sequential-template core (verified lowering templates, closed enums, plug safety-independence, no writer-authorable invariants) is genuinely strong under this lens — callback reentrancy is structurally dead (no closures, no globals, no borrows in data) and affine tokens make several classically forgeable authorities (queue endpoints, buffer loans) nonforgeable. But the design as written rests on a checker capability it explicitly disclaims: entry tokens, cursors, shard/mutex guards, and pinned rcu/snapshot views are all values carrying resumable container authority, which the digest's own rules (no borrows in data, borrow consumed by call, no reborrow) make untypeable — and every index-only workaround reopens textbook invalidation UAF through the safe facade. The single change that matters most is one new first-class kernel judgment: a pinning affine token that freezes its container binding while live (issue/consume pairs for entry/fill, cursor/next, lock/wait/unlock, pin/get), which simultaneously repairs findings 1, 3, and half of 8b, and whose rule cost must be paid honestly against the 48-rule budget. Second priority: fold fact-sheet soundness into the machine-checked template artifact and specify trap scope and io.ring teardown — until those land, the headline claims 'eleven templates are not TCB' and 'races/UAF unrepresentable' are both false at the boundary, even though every individual repair looks tractable.

design: design:schema — PARAMETERIZED FORM SCHEMAS (11 schemas + alg kit + 5 builtin-TCB facades + 2 language-primitive bundles, est. +48 spec rules)
lens: SPEC SIZE, TEACHABILITY, EVOLUTION — honest rule recount, mid-tier-writer usability from spec alone, extensibility cliff, fit with ANF/match-only/no-reborrow kernel, elegance (n forms vs n special cases)

findings.title: No-reborrow + borrow-consumed-by-call makes 'schema ops are ordinary function calls' incoherent for any multi-op sequence below owner scope
findings.detail: The digest's settled kernel rules say a unique borrow is CONSUMED by a call and there is NO reborrowing. Every schema op is specified as `op(&uniq self, ...)`. Then any function that receives `&uniq table` (or `&uniq pool`) as a parameter can perform exactly ONE op on it — the first call consumes the borrow and there is no owner in scope to re-take it from. The design's own coverage claims die on this: the LRU composition ('promote = 2-4 stores') requires table.get_uniq + pool get_uniq on THREE nodes (victim, prev, next) + link stores in one helper; the timer-wheel pattern requires bucket-pool ops in a loop inside the wheel's methods; even `Ops.push` in a loop only typechecks because the example happens to sit at owner scope. The checker_story's claim that 'the existing affine machinery handles entry tokens, guards, endpoints, and cursors without extension' is false for plain container ops — cursor works precisely because it THREADS state (returns cursor'), and no other schema does. The known fix — make every op state-in/state-out (op(uniq self, ...) -> (self, result), cursor-style threading) or add a borrow-threading return convention — is a pervasive change to all ~70 op signatures, a new language delta not in the list of 18, and a major teachability cost (every op result is now a tuple destructured by match). Evidence to settle: attempt to typecheck the design's own LRU-promote and timer-wheel compositions as helper functions under the stated v0.6 rules; they will not check. The design fails as stated; it is rescuable only by adopting uniform threading and re-costing spec size and AI-writability on that basis.

findings.title: Spec/teaching payload is undercounted roughly 3-4x: '48 rules' excludes the normative content W1 requires in context
findings.detail: The 48-rule figure counts the 18 kernel deltas generously and pushes everything else into 'catalog text' — but W1 says a modest AI writer must produce correct code from the IN-CONTEXT spec, so the catalog IS the spec for D2 purposes. Honest recount of what must be normative and in-context: (a) ~70+ op specifications across 11 schemas (seq alone lists 11 ops; each op needs ownership arrow, effect row, failure arm(s), and exported-fact row — call it 3-4 normative lines each); (b) the machine core's 'closed intrinsic enumeration' — u8x16..u64x8 is ~10 vector types crossed with lane ops/shuffles/pshufb-table/compare-mask/movemask/clmul/aes; the minimal set F5 par needs is 40-80 operations (highway ships ~300); calling this 'one rule' is dishonest counting; (c) the layout declaration grammar (~10 productions minimum, see separate finding); (d) policy enums whose ENUMERANTS carry semantics that must be taught (what utf8_range_state, clmul_quote_mask, crc32c-3way each mean and when to pick them); (e) the scenario→form decision table itself, which the ai_writability story makes load-bearing; (f) worked idiom examples per schema, which the design's own mitigation for copy-out drift requires ('one worked example per schema'). A realistic draft of this catalog is 30-60k tokens on top of the 90-rule kernel. That may still fit a modern context window — but then D2's 'spec compactness is the scarce resource' is being spent at ~4x the declared rate, and the 48-rule claim should not survive review. Evidence to settle: draft the actual normative catalog text for seq + table + bytescan + machine core alone and count tokens against kernel-spec-v0.6's size.

findings.title: Four spellings of 'bytes I own' with an unspecified conversion lattice; margin_slice is structurally unavailable exactly where zero-copy parsing matters (mmap), and the wbuf→cursor pipeline lacks a typed margin edge
findings.detail: After this design, a writer choosing a byte container faces: existing buffer<u8> (fixed, initialized), seq<u8> (growable builder), wbuf (uninit-tracked), and margin_slice views — plus box/arena. R3 ('one spelling per meaning') survives only per-op, not per-shape, and the conversion edges are where performance silently dies. Two concrete holes: (1) the single most common systems flow — recv into wbuf, freeze, decode frames with cursor — needs cursor's margin mode for the varint 8B-load trick, but the design only introduces margin_slice at seq.reserve_margin/buffer_with_margin; wbuf.freeze -> margin_slice is not specified even though the physical slop (allocated 64KB minus committed n) exists. If the edge is missing, the canonical io→parse path runs at margin=0 with per-op tail checks, or pays a defensive copy into a margin buffer — a copy on the zero-copy path. (2) filemap bytes can NEVER be margin: readable slop past a file's final page is a SIGBUS, so Σ9's fast mode is structurally incompatible with mmap'd inputs (LMDB pages, SQLite mmap mode, mold, ripgrep's mmap path — the exact workloads F6-5/F8-4 cite). Writers must therefore maintain TWO parsing disciplines (margin and non-margin) for the same format, a when-to-use hazard the spec never names, and the coverage table's PAR claims for serialization/pages implicitly assume the margin path everywhere. Evidence to settle: write the typed end-to-end pipeline for (a) 'recv length-prefixed frames and decode varints' and (b) 'walk SQLite cell pointers in a mapped file', count copies and residual per-op checks in each.

findings.title: Closed policy enums hit the extensibility cliff early, and the fallback (raw vector core) is a mid-tier performance trap; extension throughput is weeks-per-enumerant
findings.detail: The bytescan vocabulary (pred ∈ {eq1..3, range_set, nibble_table}; carry ∈ {none, clmul_quote_mask, utf8_range_state}) hard-codes specific published algorithms as spec enumerants. First realistic out-of-catalog programs arrive fast: multi-pattern/substring search (Teddy in aho-corasick/ripgrep — shuffle-bucket matching, not expressible as any listed pred), case-insensitive scanning, hex/base32 with nonstandard alphabets needing a different gather step, zstd FSE table construction, SWAR line counting with position accumulation. The prescribed fallback is the F9 vector core — machine-sufficient, but the writer must hand-reconstruct exactly what Σ7 existed to hide: unaligned head handling, 4x unroll, masked tails, and crucially over-reads are ILLEGAL on ordinary slices (margin_slice is allocation-side only, and the haystack is usually a borrowed input the writer didn't allocate), so the tail becomes a scalar loop. A mid-tier model will ship the correct-but-scalar-tail version: on 100B-1KB inputs (headers, tokens) that is a 2-5x loss with zero compile-time signal — the exact 'par decays silently' failure the design fears from copy-out drift, but on the hot F5 family. Meanwhile the repair path is heavyweight: a new enumerant means template re-proof (E1 budgets 4 person-weeks for ONE template) plus per-parameter fact-sheet hostile review, so catalog findings clear in weeks. Evidence to settle: pilot-port ripgrep's core (memchr + memmem + Teddy) and one codec; count out-of-enum findings in month one and measure the vector-core fallback written by a mid-tier model against memchr-crate memmem.

findings.title: The layout sublanguage is a hidden second language: fixed-offset fields cover toy headers, while the cited real targets (SQLite pages, ELF, protobuf submessages) need data-dependent structure the grammar doesn't have
findings.detail: Σ8's grammar as sketched (field offset/width/endianness/alignment, 'length-prefixed sections') expresses a TCP fixed header. The workloads the coverage table cites for its PAR verdict do not fit: a SQLite b-tree leaf page has a header-count-prefixed big-endian u16 cell-pointer ARRAY whose entries are OFFSETS to variable-length cells elsewhere in the page; ELF has section tables indexing into the file; capnp/flatbuffers are offset GRAPHS with bounds obligations per pointer. Expressing these needs repetition, data-dependent offsets, cross-field arithmetic, and intra-buffer pointer validation — i.e., the grammar converges on Kaitai-struct-class complexity, easily 10-20 additional kernel-adjacent rules, or it stays fixed-layout-only and every real format routes to 'cursor + once-proven bounds facts', a composition the design names as its thinnest part and never demonstrates (which fact classes let a validated cell-pointer read become residual-check-free? that is a NEW fact channel per format family, each requiring hostile review per standing rule). Either branch breaks a stated claim: spec compactness (D2) or the zero-copy PAR verdicts on pages/records. Evidence to settle: write the actual layout declaration for a SQLite leaf page and the fact derivation making one cell access 1-load; if it cannot be written, downgrade F5-6 and the SQLite-adjacent rows to PARTIAL.

findings.title: Shape-level when-to-use ambiguity: three concurrent-map spellings, two node-graph substrates, and a builder/wbuf split give a mid-tier model real wrong-form rates the decision table cannot fully absorb
findings.detail: R3 holds per-op but not per-problem. Concrete confusion pairs a mid-tier model faces from the spec alone: (a) shard<table> vs rcu_table vs mutex<table> — the correct pick depends on read/write ratio and whether values are frozen, a quantitative judgment from prose; wrong pick (rcu_table under write churn, or mutex<table> for a hot read path) compiles, is safe, and loses 5-20x under contention with no signal; (b) pool vs region for a navigable AST — both coverage rows claim tree par; region gives real references but no individual free and no stable cross-iteration identity, and the wall appears late (when the first detach/reattach is needed), forcing a rewrite; (c) seq<u8> builder vs wbuf for output assembly — the correct rule (wbuf only when an EXTERNAL writer fills bytes) is stated nowhere; (d) ring vs conc_queue<spsc> for 'single-threaded today, maybe threaded later'. The design's decision table is the right mitigation, but it is itself unwritten spec payload (see the recount finding), and the copy-out-drift lint it proposes covers borrow errors, not form-selection errors. Evidence to settle: the obvious W1 eval — give a mid-tier model the drafted spec plus 20 scenario prompts sampled from the demand map and measure wrong-form selection rate; >10-15% on high-weight scenarios means the catalog needs merging (e.g., one concurrent-map entry with a policy knob), not more prose.

findings.title: The design's own source examples don't typecheck in the stated language: `?` propagation, expression nesting, and closure-shaped plugs presuppose kernel features that don't exist, and the missing error-propagation form is an unlisted delta
findings.detail: source_shape lines use `cursor.varint_u64(c0)?` (no `?` exists; match-only), nested calls like `Ops.push(&uniq v, op)` inside loop headers (ANF forbids expression nesting), and plug-points written as if inline values (`hasher=foldhash` is fine as an enum, but sort comparators/extractors/view_eq are env-struct + contract ceremonies under P5 — a 'one-line sort call' is actually a top-level struct, a contract impl, and an instantiation). Individually these are presentation slips, but they reveal an unexamined fit cost: a 10-field protobuf decoder under match-only error handling with no `?`-equivalent is 10 levels of match nesting or 10 early-return matches — the exact deep-nesting shape mid-tier models corrupt (wrong arm, dropped cursor rebind). Since Σ9 threads an affine cursor through every op, a single blessed 'propagate-error-and-rebind' form (one spelling, per R3) is effectively mandatory for the cursor schema to be writable, and it is absent from the 18 language deltas. Evidence to settle: hand-elaborate the design's own cursor example into legal v0.6 ANF/match form and hand it to a mid-tier model to extend by three fields; count corruption rate with and without a propagation form.

overall: The stance is rescuable and is probably the right family of answer — schemas genuinely deliver many containers from few spec objects, and the TCB discipline is the best-argued part — but it is not shippable as stated. One finding is fatal as written: every schema op is spelled `&uniq self` in a kernel where a unique borrow is consumed by a call and reborrowing does not exist, so any helper function can perform exactly one op on a container and the design's own LRU/timer-wheel/multi-node compositions do not typecheck; the fix (uniform cursor-style state-threading for all ops) is known but changes ~70 signatures, adds an unlisted delta, and must be adopted before any other claim is evaluated. The single most important change is therefore: rewrite the op tables in honest borrow-threading form, then re-run the spec-size and AI-writability accounting on that basis — because the second-order problems (a real catalog payload ~3-4x the claimed 48 rules, the four-way byte-buffer lattice with missing wbuf→margin and impossible filemap→margin edges, the early out-of-enum cliff whose vector-core fallback is a silent 2-5x trap, and a layout grammar that either grows to Kaitai-class or forfeits its PAR verdicts) are all costed against the current, understated version of the design. If the threading rewrite plus an honest token-counted catalog draft still fit D2 and pass a W1 wrong-form eval, the schema stance stands; the closed-enum and margin-provenance findings then become manageable catalog-process work rather than design refutations.
